title: unable to run container image; SELinux denial
body:
/kind bug

I have a pre-built container image that I have been using as a "pet container" for some time now.  Currently unable to run the container due to an SELinux denial.  This looks similar to #2759.  This is on Fedora Silverblue 29.

```
$ rpm -qa | grep -e container-selinux -e podman -e runc
runc-1.0.0-85.dev.gitdd22a84.fc29.x86_64
container-selinux-2.95-1.gite3ebc68.fc29.noarch
podman-1.2.0-2.git3bd528e.fc29.x86_64

$ sudo podman images | grep myprecious
localhost/miabbott/myprecious                                                    29          019e1deed255   6 weeks ago    2.73 GB
docker-registry-default.cloud.registry.upshift.redhat.com/miabbott/myprecious    29          019e1deed255   6 weeks ago    2.73 GB

$ sudo podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE  COMMAND  CREATED  STATUS  PORTS  NAMES

$ sudo podman run --net=host --rm -it --privileged localhost/miabbott/myprecious:29
Error: error reading container (probably exited) json message: EOF

$ sudo podman --log-level=debug run --net=host --rm -it --privileged localhost/miabbott/myprecious:29                                                                                     DEBU[0000] Initializing boltdb state at /var/lib/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db
```

comments.body:
I have to throw something up on the troubleshooting guide to try without `-t` to get better error messages..

Still, that is definitely an AVC coming off runc, so I think you're entirely correct that this is SELinux

> Still, that is definitely an AVC coming off runc, so I think you're entirely correct that this is SELinux

Matches up with the packages that were upgraded between my two deployments:

```
$ rpm-ostree db diff 9635c6a89dd61ece0db0650a7665ff1fec33c455ae3c9fd1f63bd7e5371ff193 28fc7054954e90ab1b4fdc251ab412d17be6c9de70c76aecc471ccaecf63a033 | grep -e runc -e podman -e container-selinux
  container-selinux 2:2.94-1.git1e99f1d.fc29 -> 2:2.95-1.gite3ebc68.fc29
  runc 2:1.0.0-68.dev.git6635b4f.fc29 -> 2:1.0.0-85.dev.gitdd22a84.fc29
```

@mheon should I move this over to `runc`?
I'll leave that one to @rhatdan to decide
I downgraded to the previous version of `runc` (runc-1.0.0-68.dev.git6635b4f.fc29.x86_64) and was able to get around this.

How is podman and runc labeled?  `ls -lZ /usr/bin/podman /usr/bin/runc`

The AVC shows that you are running the command as unconfined_t, when the command should be running as container_runtime_t. 

This usually means that runc or podman are mislabeled.

They should be labeled container_runtime_exec_t.

ls -lZ /usr/bin/podman /usr/sbin/runc 
-rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root system_u:object_r:container_runtime_exec_t:s0 52343416 Apr  1 10:34 /usr/bin/podman
-rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root system_u:object_r:container_runtime_exec_t:s0 11837032 Mar 20 22:21 /usr/sbin/runc

It looks like the fix to allow this was in 2.01 

```
diff --git a/VERSION b/VERSION
index 1a73c77..7cd6b11 100644
--- a/VERSION
+++ b/VERSION
@@ -1 +1 @@
-2.90
+2.91
diff --git a/container.te b/container.te
index 118533c..01315e7 100644
--- a/container.te
+++ b/container.te
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
-policy_module(container, 2.90.0)
+policy_module(container, 2.91.0)
 gen_require(`
        class passwd rootok;
 ')
@@ -510,6 +510,7 @@ optional_policy(`
        role_transition unconfined_r container_runtime_exec_t system_r;
        allow container_runtime_t unconfined_t:fifo_file setattr;
        allow unconfined_t container_domain:process dyntransition;
+       allow unconfined_t unlabeled_t:key manage_key_perms;
 ')
 ...
```

This indicates to me that container-selinux was not successfully installed on this system

@rhatdan Strange, my binaries are labeled properly:

```
$ rpm -q podman runc
podman-1.2.0-2.git3bd528e.fc29.x86_64
runc-1.0.0-85.dev.gitdd22a84.fc29.x86_64

$ ls -lZ /usr/bin/podman /usr/bin/runc
-rwxr-xr-x. 4 root root system_u:object_r:container_runtime_exec_t:s0 49614968 Dec 31  1969 /usr/bin/podman
-rwxr-xr-x. 3 root root system_u:object_r:container_runtime_exec_t:s0  8952232 Dec 31  1969 /usr/bin/runc
```

Stranger still, I'm unable to reproduce this in a VM that has the same installed versions.

So...I guess that means I managed to muck up my SELinux labels on my host.  :sob: 

I'll close this since it is not reproducible.
The other issue could be if /usr was mounted nusuid, this could block transitions.
